result:
# The Weight of Ordinary Days

There's a Tuesday in everyone's life that changes everything, and you never know it's a Tuesday when it starts.

Mine smelled like burnt coffee and rain on asphalt. I remember that specifically because I've tried so hard to remember everything about the before, to pin it down like a butterfly under glass, to say *here, this is where the world was still whole.* But memory doesn't work that way. It gives you burnt coffee and rain, and it takes away what shirt you were wearing, what song was on the radio, whether you said "I love you" or just "see you later" on your way out the door.

I think about that a lot—the way we treat ordinary moments like they're infinite. Like there will always be another Tuesday morning, another cup of coffee, another chance to say the thing you mean instead of the thing that's easy.

---

My grandmother used to keep a jar of buttons on her windowsill. Not for any practical reason. She just liked them. She'd run her fingers through them while she talked, and they'd make this sound like distant rain, like a tiny applause. When she died, nobody wanted the buttons. They weren't valuable. They weren't even beautiful, not really—just plastic circles in faded colors, chipped and scratched from years of being touched.

I took them.

They sit on my windowsill now, and sometimes I run my fingers through them, and I can almost hear her voice underneath that small rattling. She used to say that people spend so much time building monuments when what they should be building is moments. She said this while doing absolutely nothing remarkable—sitting in a kitchen chair, watching light move across a table, holding buttons that meant nothing and everything simultaneously.

I didn't understand her then. I think I'm starting to now.

---

Here's what I've learned about grief, since we're here and being honest: it doesn't move in stages. The textbooks lied, or at least oversimplified, which might be the same thing. Grief moves like weather. It moves like a tide that ignores your calendar. You'll be standing in a grocery store, holding a bag of oranges, and suddenly you can't breathe because someone nearby is wearing a perfume that smells like a person who doesn't exist anymore. And then it passes. And then you buy the oranges. And then you go home and eat one, and it's good, and you feel guilty for noticing that it's good, and then you feel tired of feeling guilty, and then you just sit there, with sticky fingers and an orange peel curling on the counter, being alive in the specific way that only people who've lost someone can be alive—which is to say, reluctantly and gratefully at the same time.

---

I've been thinking about how we talk to each other. How we've built all these technologies—these glowing rectangles we carry in our pockets, these networks spanning oceans—and we still can't say what we mean. We text "lol" when we're not laughing. We say "I'm fine" like a locked door. We post photographs of sunsets as proxies for the feelings we can't articulate, hoping someone out there will look at the same orange sky and *know.*

Sometimes they do. That's the miracle we don't talk about enough—that sometimes, across all that distance and static, someone actually hears you. Not your words, but you. The thing underneath the words. The breath before the sentence.

My friend called me last week and said, "I don't need anything, I just wanted to hear your voice." And I stood in my kitchen, holding the phone, and cried. Not because I was sad. Because being known is so rare and so terrifying and so necessary that when it happens, your body doesn't know what to do except the most honest thing it can, which is weep.

---

I don't know what this is. An essay, maybe. A letter to no one. A jar of buttons.

I think what I'm trying to say is this: the ordinary things are not ordinary. The Tuesday morning coffee. The sound of someone breathing next to you in the dark. The way a dog rests its head on your foot like you are the whole world, and for a moment you believe it because the dog seems so sure.

We keep waiting for our lives to start—after the promotion, after the move, after we lose the weight or find the person or fix the thing that's broken. But the life is happening now, in the waiting. The life *is* the waiting. It's the weight of ordinary days stacked one on top of another, and it is unspeakably beautiful if you can stay still long enough to notice.

My grandmother knew this. She sat with her buttons and her window light and she knew.

I'm trying to know it too. Most days I fail. I scroll, I rush, I forget to look up. But sometimes—in the grocery store, holding oranges, or on the phone hearing a voice that knows me—sometimes I catch it. A flash of the sacred hiding inside the mundane, like light through a crack in a door.

And I think: *this. Don't forget this._

I will forget. I always do.

But the buttons are on my windowsill, waiting to remind me.
